healthy_eating.consult_a_dietitian_about:
I would like to talk with the dietitian about how to build a realistic eating plan that fits my schedule as a full-time middle school teacher and a single parent of two kids. Specifically, I want help with:

1. PORTION SIZES AND CALORIE TARGETS:
- Understanding what a healthy 1,700–1,800 calorie day actually looks like on my plate, broken down into 3 meals and 1–2 snacks.
- Getting clear examples of portion sizes for common foods I actually eat (e.g., how much rice or pasta should I serve myself, what does 3–4 oz of chicken look like without a scale, etc.).

2. MEAL PLANNING WITH LIMITED TIME:
- How to plan 3–4 simple dinners that I can rotate during the week that are kid-friendly but still support my weight-loss goals (for example, baked chicken thighs with roasted vegetables and brown rice, turkey tacos with lettuce and tomato, and a slow-cooker chili with beans and lean ground turkey).
- Ideas for quick breakfasts I can eat in the car or at my desk before classes start (like Greek yogurt with berries and a handful of nuts, or a whole-grain English muffin with peanut butter and a banana).

3. EMOTIONAL AND EVENING EATING:
- Strategies for dealing with my pattern of snacking on chips, cookies, and ice cream after 8:00 PM when I’m grading papers or watching TV. I’d like alternatives that still feel comforting (like air-popped popcorn, herbal tea with a small piece of dark chocolate, or sliced apples with a tablespoon of almond butter).
- How to handle cravings around my menstrual cycle when I feel especially drawn to salty and sweet foods.

4. EATING OUT AND SOCIAL EVENTS:
- Guidance on what to order at common places I go with colleagues or my kids (pizza places, Mexican restaurants, school events with catered sandwiches and cookies), and how to manage portions without feeling deprived.
- How to plan ahead on days when I know there will be a staff lunch, birthday celebration, or a school event with lots of snacks.

5. MACRONUTRIENT BALANCE:
- How much protein, carbohydrates, and fat I should aim for per meal, considering I am on a GLP-1 medication and get full faster.
- Whether I should be limiting carbs strictly or just focusing on higher-fiber, slower-digesting carbs like beans, lentils, oats, and whole grains.

6. FAMILY AND BUDGET CONSIDERATIONS:
- Ways to cook one main meal that works for both me and my kids, without making separate “diet food” for myself.
- Budget-friendly ideas, since I’m trying to keep groceries at a reasonable cost (for example, using more frozen vegetables, canned beans, and store-brand items).

7. TRACKING AND ACCOUNTABILITY:
- Whether I should be using a food tracking app every day or just a few days a week to spot patterns.
- How to recognize when tracking becomes obsessive versus helpful.

8. SPECIAL CONCERNS:
- I have mild high blood pressure and prediabetes, so I’d like specific guidance on sodium limits, added sugars, and how often I can have things like desserts or fast food while still making progress.

I want to leave the appointment with at least two sample daily meal plans, a simple grocery list, and a short list of “go-to” meals and snacks that I can rely on when I’m stressed or very busy.
healthy_eating.notes:
Persona details for context: My name is Sarah Martinez, I’m 42 years old, 5'5" tall, and currently weigh around 228 lbs. I work full-time as a 7th-grade English teacher at a public middle school. I live in Aurora, Colorado, with my two kids (ages 9 and 12). My primary health goals are to lose about 50–60 lbs over the next 1–2 years, reduce my blood pressure (currently borderline high), and prevent my prediabetes from progressing to type 2 diabetes.

Eating habits and goals:
- I tend to skip breakfast or just have coffee, then I’m starving by lunchtime and overeat. One of my main goals is to eat a small but balanced breakfast every day within 1–2 hours of waking.
- I want to consistently pack my lunch for school at least 4 days per week instead of relying on the cafeteria or fast food.
- I’m aiming for a daily calorie range of about 1,700–1,800 calories, as recommended by my provider, with a focus on high-protein, higher-fiber meals that keep me full.
- I plan to limit soda to once per week (I used to drink 1–2 cans of regular cola a day). I’ve already switched to flavored sparkling water most days.
- I’m trying to follow the “half plate vegetables, quarter lean protein, quarter whole grains or starchy vegetables” approach at dinner.

Specific changes I’m committing to in the next 3 months:
1. Breakfast: Have something with protein every morning (examples: Greek yogurt with fruit; scrambled eggs with spinach; protein shake with a banana and peanut butter).
2. Lunch: Pack a lunch at home at least Monday–Thursday. Typical options: salad with grilled chicken and chickpeas; turkey and veggie sandwich on whole-grain bread with carrot sticks; leftovers from dinner.
3. Dinner: Cook at home at least 5 nights per week. Keep frozen vegetables on hand for quick sides and use the air fryer for chicken and fish to reduce added oils.
4. Snacks: Keep pre-portioned nuts, string cheese, baby carrots, and hummus ready in the fridge to avoid grabbing chips and cookies.
5. Drinks: Aim for at least 64 oz (about 8 cups) of water or unsweetened beverages daily. Limit alcohol to no more than 1 drink per week.

I know I tend to fall off track when I’m exhausted from work or dealing with stress from parenting and grading. To prepare for this, I’ve written out a short list of “backup meals” for those nights, like rotisserie chicken with microwaveable brown rice and a bagged salad, or whole-wheat pasta with jarred marinara sauce and a side of frozen broccoli.

Overall, my focus is on consistency and sustainability rather than perfection. I’m trying to avoid all-or-nothing thinking (for example, seeing one indulgent meal as a total failure) and instead treat each meal as a new opportunity to make a better choice.

physical_activity.other_activity:
I plan to start doing low-impact workout videos at home (20–30 minutes) 3 times per week using YouTube. These will mostly be beginner-friendly cardio and light strength routines that don’t require special equipment beyond a yoga mat and a pair of dumbbells. I like following along with guided videos because they keep me motivated and take the guesswork out of what exercises to do.

Given my schedule as a teacher and a single parent, I’m focusing on activities that I can do at or near home without needing a gym membership. Specifically:
- 20–30 minute indoor walking or low-impact cardio videos on school nights, after dinner but before 8:00 PM.
- 10–15 minute stretching or gentle yoga before bed on nights when I feel especially tense or have been on my feet all day.
- Occasional weekend bike rides with my kids on the trail near our house, aiming for 30–45 minutes at a comfortable pace.

I also plan to build more movement into my regular day by:
- Parking farther away from the school entrance and taking the stairs instead of the elevator.
- Walking laps inside the school or around the block during my planning period or lunch break at least 2–3 times a week.

My long-term goal is to work up to at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity per week, plus 2–3 days of strength training, but I’m starting smaller so I don’t get overwhelmed or injured.

behavior_and_mindset.focus_on_small_changes_like:
I am focusing on a few small but meaningful behavior and mindset changes that feel realistic with my life as a 42-year-old teacher and mom:

1. PAUSING BEFORE I EAT:
- I will take 10–20 seconds before eating anything (especially snacks) to ask myself, “Am I physically hungry, or am I stressed, bored, or tired?” If I’m not truly hungry, I’ll try another coping strategy first, like drinking a glass of water, taking a few deep breaths, or stepping away from my desk for a short walk.

2. SLOWING DOWN MEALS:
- I tend to eat very quickly between classes or while distracted by my phone or grading. I’m going to practice putting my fork down between bites and taking at least 15–20 minutes to finish my meals, especially dinner. I’ll also try to eat at the table instead of in front of the TV at least 5 nights a week.

3. PLANNING AHEAD FOR TRIGGER TIMES:
- My toughest time of day is between 8:00–10:00 PM, when the kids are in bed and I finally sit down. I often mindlessly snack on chips, cookies, or ice cream during this window. My small change is to:
  - Set a “kitchen closed” time at 9:00 PM.
  - Plan one intentional evening snack (like a cup of herbal tea and a piece of fruit, or a small bowl of air-popped popcorn) instead of grazing.

4. USING A SIMPLE FOOD AND ACTIVITY JOURNAL:
- I’m committing to logging what I eat and my activity at least 5 days per week for the next 2 months, using a basic app on my phone. I’ll also jot down how I’m feeling emotionally when I have strong cravings or episodes of overeating, so I can start to see patterns (like stress after parent-teacher conferences or loneliness on Sunday nights).

5. PREPARING FOR SETBACKS IN ADVANCE:
- Instead of assuming I’ll be “perfect,” I’m writing out a short plan for what I’ll do after a day of overeating or skipping workouts. My rule will be: “Next meal, back to basics,” rather than waiting until the next Monday or the start of a new month. I’ll remind myself that one off day doesn’t erase my overall progress.

6. IMPROVING SLEEP HABITS:
- I notice I snack more and crave sugary foods when I’m exhausted. I’m aiming for a consistent bedtime of around 10:30 PM on school nights and waking up at 6:00 AM. To support this, I’m setting a phone alarm for 10:00 PM as a signal to start winding down, turning off screens, and doing a short stretching or breathing routine.

7. POSITIVE SELF-TALK AND REALISTIC EXPECTATIONS:
- I’ve struggled with negative self-talk about my weight for years. I’m practicing talking to myself the way I would talk to one of my students—encouraging, patient, and focused on effort rather than perfection. I’m also setting realistic expectations: aiming for 1–2 lbs of weight loss per week on average, and accepting that some weeks the scale may not move even if I’m doing the right things.

8. BUILDING A SUPPORT SYSTEM:
- I’ve told my sister and one close coworker about my goals so they can check in with me and offer support. I’m also considering joining an online support group for people working on weight management.

These small changes are all things I can start right away without needing special equipment or extra money. They’re focused on building awareness, slowing down, and being kinder to myself, which I believe will help me stick with my plan long term.
